title: Five-Minute Mindfulness
image_idea: calm misty lake or forest at dawn, low contrast, no people

segments.text: Find a position that feels comfortable, and let yourself settle into it.
You can close your eyes, or just let your gaze soften and rest on a point in front of you.
There's nothing to achieve here. Nowhere else to be for the next few minutes. Just this.
Begin to notice that you're breathing. You don't need to change it. Just notice it.
Feel where the breath is most obvious to you. Maybe the cool air at your nostrils. Maybe the rise and fall of your chest, or your belly.
Rest your attention there. On one breath.
And the next.
At some point, maybe already, you'll notice your attention has drifted. A thought, a sound, a plan for later.
That's completely fine. That's what minds do.
When you notice you've wandered, that noticing is the practice. Gently bring your attention back to the breath. No judgment. Just begin again.
Now let your awareness widen to include your whole body, sitting here.
Notice the points where you make contact with the floor, or the chair. The weight of your body being held.
If there's tension anywhere, the shoulders, the jaw, the hands, see if it can soften, just a little.
For the next while, simply rest.
You don't need to follow the breath closely now. Just sit, and be aware of whatever is here. Sounds, sensations, the breath moving on its own.
Letting everything be exactly as it is.
Begin to bring your attention back to the room around you.
Notice the sounds of the space you're in. Feel the surface beneath you.
When you're ready, let your eyes open.
Take this sense of calm with you. The breath is always here, whenever you want to return to it.
And that brings our practice to a close. The meditation is complete. When you're ready, carry this calm with you, and go gently into the rest of your day.
